skills.skill: 4.1 Interpreting the Meaning of the Derivative in Context
skills.prompt: This topic covers interpreting derivatives in real-world contexts for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.1). When f(t) represents a quantity in context (population, temperature, cost, etc.), f'(t) represents the instantaneous rate of change of that quantity with respect to t. Students must: (1) state what f'(t) represents in the context (e.g., 'the rate at which the population is changing at time t'), (2) include correct units (units of f divided by units of t), and (3) interpret the sign (positive means increasing, negative means decreasing). Key concepts: the derivative provides local information about how fast and in which direction a quantity is changing; the value f'(a) approximates the change in f for a small change in the input near a. AP exam note: free-response questions consistently require contextual interpretation with correct units. Stating 'the derivative is the rate of change' without specifying what is changing and with respect to what loses points. Vocabulary: rate of change, context, units, interpretation, increasing, decreasing, instantaneous.

skills.skill: 4.2 Straight-Line Motion: Connecting Position, Velocity, and Acceleration
skills.prompt: This topic covers straight-line (rectilinear) motion for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.2). If s(t) is the position of a particle at time t, then v(t) = s'(t) is velocity and a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t) is acceleration. Key concepts: velocity is positive when the particle moves in the positive direction and negative when moving in the negative direction; speed is |v(t)|; the particle changes direction when velocity changes sign (v(t) = 0 and v changes sign); the particle speeds up when velocity and acceleration have the same sign and slows down when they have opposite signs. Students should analyze motion from position, velocity, or acceleration functions/graphs. Displacement over [a,b] is s(b) - s(a); total distance traveled requires considering direction changes. AP exam note: particle motion is one of the most common free-response topics. Students must clearly distinguish between displacement and total distance. Vocabulary: position, velocity, acceleration, speed, displacement, distance traveled, direction change.

skills.skill: 4.3 Rates of Change in Applied Contexts Other Than Motion
skills.prompt: This topic covers applying derivatives to rates of change in non-motion contexts for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.C, Unit 4, Topic 4.3). Students interpret derivatives in diverse contexts: population growth (dP/dt), temperature change (dT/dt), cost functions (marginal cost dC/dx), volume change (dV/dt), and more. Key concepts: identifying the independent and dependent variables, computing rates using differentiation rules, interpreting the sign and magnitude of the derivative in context, and including appropriate units. For example, if C(x) = 0.01x^2 + 5x + 100 represents cost in dollars for x items, then C'(x) = 0.02x + 5 gives the marginal cost (approximate cost of producing one additional item) in dollars per item. Students should connect the derivative to the behavior of the function (increasing/decreasing, fast/slow change). AP exam note: contextual interpretation with units is critical on free-response questions. Students should practice writing complete sentences interpreting derivatives. Vocabulary: applied rate of change, marginal cost, units, interpretation, context, independent variable, dependent variable.

This topic introduces related rates problems for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.D, Unit 4, Topic 4.4). Related rates problems involve two or more quantities that change over time and are connected by an equation. By differentiating the equation with respect to time t (using implicit differentiation and the Chain Rule), students relate the rates of change of the quantities. Key steps: (1) draw a diagram and identify variables, (2) write an equation relating the variables, (3) differentiate both sides with respect to t, (4) substitute known values and solve for the unknown rate. Example: for a circle with increasing radius, A = pi*r^2 gives dA/dt = 2*pi*r*(dr/dt). Students must distinguish between variables (which change with time) and constants. Common relationships come from geometry (Pythagorean theorem, volume formulas, similar triangles, trigonometry). AP exam note: related rates is a classic free-response topic. Students must clearly show the differentiation step and substitute only after differentiating. Vocabulary: related rates, implicit differentiation with respect to time, rate of change, variables, constants.

This topic covers solving related rates problems for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.E, Unit 4, Topic 4.5). Building on Topic 4.4, students solve more complex related rates problems involving various geometric and physical scenarios. Common problem types: expanding/contracting circles and spheres, filling/draining tanks (cones, cylinders), ladder problems (Pythagorean theorem), shadow problems (similar triangles), and angle of elevation problems (trigonometry). Key strategies: choose the equation that directly relates the quantities whose rates you know and want to find; eliminate extra variables using geometric relationships before differentiating; pay attention to signs (negative rates for decreasing quantities). Students should express the final answer with correct units and interpret it in context. AP exam note: a common error is substituting known values before differentiating, which incorrectly treats variables as constants. Students must differentiate first, then substitute. Another error is using the wrong geometric formula. Vocabulary: related rates, geometric relationships, Pythagorean theorem, similar triangles, volume, surface area, rate.

skills.skill: 4.6 Approximating Values of a Function Using Local Linearity and Linearization
skills.prompt: This topic covers local linearity and linearization for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-3.F, Unit 4, Topic 4.6). The tangent line at x = a, L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a), provides a linear approximation of f(x) near x = a. This works because differentiable functions are approximately linear when zoomed in sufficiently (local linearity). Key concepts: the linearization L(x) is the best linear approximation near x = a; the approximation f(x) ~ L(x) is better for x closer to a; if f is concave up (f'' > 0), the linearization underestimates f; if concave down (f'' < 0), it overestimates. Example: approximate sqrt(4.1) using f(x) = sqrt(x) at a = 4: L(x) = 2 + (1/4)(x-4), so sqrt(4.1) ~ 2.025. This concept connects to differentials: dy = f'(x)dx approximates the change in f. AP exam note: linearization appears in both multiple-choice and free-response contexts. Students should state whether the approximation is an over- or underestimate using concavity. Vocabulary: linearization, tangent line approximation, local linearity, overestimate, underestimate, concavity, differential.

skills.skill: 4.7 Using L’Hospital’s Rule for Determining Limits of Indeterminate Forms
skills.prompt: This topic covers L'Hopital's Rule for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-4.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.7). L'Hopital's Rule states: if lim[x->c] f(x)/g(x) yields the indeterminate form 0/0 or infinity/infinity, and f and g are differentiable near c with g'(x) != 0 near c, then lim[x->c] f(x)/g(x) = lim[x->c] f'(x)/g'(x), provided the latter limit exists or is infinite. Key concepts: the rule only applies to indeterminate forms 0/0 or infinity/infinity; other indeterminate forms (0*infinity, infinity-infinity, 0^0, 1^infinity, infinity^0) must be rewritten into 0/0 or infinity/infinity form first; the rule can be applied repeatedly if needed; always verify the indeterminate form before applying. Example: lim[x->0] sin(x)/x = lim[x->0] cos(x)/1 = 1. AP exam note: students must verify the indeterminate form to receive full credit. Using L'Hopital's Rule on non-indeterminate forms gives incorrect results. Vocabulary: L'Hopital's Rule, indeterminate form, 0/0, infinity/infinity, differentiable, verify conditions.
